our_argument_summary: Section 3.1 argues that the dome's Live Power dashboard claims 9.2σ convergence across 20 independent domains, but 14 of those 20 domains share the same fitted constant λ_g = 8,619 km, making them one test repeated 14 times rather than 14 independent tests. The remaining 6 domains are classified as: 3 known constants claimed as predictions (Group B), and 3 potentially testable but problematic (Group C). The bottom line claims zero of 20 domains produce a result where the globe disagrees and the dome uniquely explains the observation.

holes_found.description: BOTTOM LINE CONTRADICTS TABLE ENTRY FOR POLARIS. The bottom line states: 'Of the remaining 6, the globe predicts the same or better in 5, and the dome is contradicted by data in 1 (Polaris).' But the table entry for Domain #17 (Polaris) says: 'Neither model is tested by this; the measurement isn't precise enough to discriminate.' These two statements are directly contradictory. The table says Polaris doesn't test either model; the summary says it contradicts the dome. A dome defender would immediately seize on this: 'Your own analysis says Polaris doesn't discriminate, then your summary claims it refutes us. Which is it?' This undermines the section's credibility at its most quotable point.
holes_found.recommendation: Align the bottom line with the table. Either (a) strengthen the Polaris table entry to explain why the dome IS contradicted (e.g., the V12 prediction PRED-NEW-001 claims 3-7° excess which IS refuted by centuries of navigation), or (b) change the bottom line to: 'Of the remaining 6, the globe predicts the same or better in 5, and the 6th (Polaris) fails to discriminate.' Option (a) is stronger — the V12 prediction of 3-7° excess at high latitudes is genuinely refuted by every sextant reading since the 18th century, but the table doesn't mention this.

holes_found.description: NO RAW TEXT OF DOME'S LIVE POWER PAGE EXISTS IN REPOSITORY. The section critiques 'The Live Power page' and its 20 domains, but the raw-text/ directory contains no extraction of this page. All dome site extractions are from V50.6 (2026-03-12). The Live Power dashboard is described as a V51.0 addition. This means our classification of all 20 domains into Groups A/B/C — the backbone of the entire section — cannot be verified against the dome's actual page. A dome defender could say: 'You're critiquing a page you haven't even read properly. Your domain assignments are wrong because you're working from memory, not from the source.' We should extract and archive the Live Power page.
holes_found.recommendation: Extract the Live Power page from john09289.github.io/predictions/ and add it to raw-text/v51.0-2026-04-05/. This provides a citable, archived source for every claim in Section 3.1. Until this is done, the section's domain-by-domain analysis rests on unverifiable characterizations.

holes_found.description: THE 'FITTED CONSTANT' FRAMING IS IMPRECISE. We call λ_g = 8,619 km a 'fitted constant' and say 'Testing whether a fitted constant reproduces the data it was fitted to is not a prediction — it is a tautology.' But we never explain HOW λ_g was fitted or to WHAT data. The dome claims all parameters are 'physically derived or observationally constrained' and uses zero fitted parameters. The dome's own model page says H(r) = 8537 × exp(-r/8619) 'reconciles three previously contradictory H measurements' — this IS fitting (3 data points, 2 free parameters). But our section never makes this explicit. A defender would say: 'λ_g is derived from first principles of dome geometry, not curve-fitted to the 14 domains you list.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence after 'This single constant was fitted to geomagnetic data' explaining the fitting: 'The dome's own model page states H(r) reconciles three H measurements (Schumann ~9,500 km, Polaris geometric 4,750 km, parameterization 9,086 km). Fitting a two-parameter exponential (H₀, λ_g) to three data points is classical curve fitting with one degree of freedom. The resulting λ_g then appears in the 14 domains below, but it was not derived independently of them.' This preempts the 'first principles' defense.

holes_found.description: STATISTICAL INDEPENDENCE CLAIM IS OVERSIMPLIFIED. We state that shared parameters destroy statistical independence. This is true in the naive chi-square sense, but a sophisticated defender could argue: 'In physics, G appears in many equations. That doesn't mean tests of gravity in different regimes are not independent. Each domain tests a DIFFERENT equation that uses λ_g. The independence is in the functional form, not the parameter.' Our ruler analogy ('measuring height in 30 rooms') is effective rhetoric but doesn't address this objection. The real argument is stronger: λ_g was fitted to observations from these SAME domains, so new measurements from these domains cannot independently confirm the fit.
holes_found.recommendation: Sharpen the distinction: 'The issue is not that multiple equations share a constant — that is normal in physics (G, c, ℏ all appear in many formulas). The issue is that λ_g was fitted to data from these same domains. When G was measured from gravitational experiments, it then correctly predicted unrelated phenomena (precession of Mercury, gravitational lensing). λ_g was fitted to Schumann, geomagnetic, and gravity data, then 'tested' against Schumann, geomagnetic, and gravity data. There is no out-of-sample prediction.' This is a much stronger argument than the ruler analogy.

holes_found.description: DOMAIN #2 (TESLA) — GLOBE PREDICTS SAME COLUMN SAYS 'YES (spherical propagation)' BUT THE PROBLEM COLUMN SAYS TESLA'S MEASUREMENT ASSUMES SPHERICAL EARTH. These are slightly different claims. Saying 'Globe Predicts Same' implies the globe independently arrives at the same value; saying 'Tesla assumed spherical Earth' implies the dome's value is DERIVED FROM globe geometry. The latter is the stronger argument (the dome didn't independently derive the Tesla frequency — it took Tesla's globe-based measurement and reinterpreted it). The column entry undersells this. Additionally, our WIN-001 review established that Tesla patent 787412 doesn't actually contain the cited formula f=c/(2D). If this finding is correct, Domain #2 should reference it.
holes_found.recommendation: Change the 'Globe Predicts Same?' column for #2 to 'YES (derived from it)' or similar, and add a note: 'Tesla patent 787412 does not contain the cited formula (see WIN-001). The 11.78 Hz value is derived from spherical-Earth propagation time.' This connects the section-level analysis to the WIN-level evidence.

holes_found.description: THE SECTION DOESN'T REFERENCE code_analysis TAG DATA. The curmudgeon has now reviewed 58/67 WINs with structural tags. The section claims 14 domains share λ_g, but individual WIN reviews tagged each WIN for 'derives_from_dome.' Only 4/58 reviewed WINs derive from dome geometry. This tag data directly supports the section's argument but is not cited. Similarly, 35/58 relabel_standard and 46/58 post_hoc — these per-WIN structural findings reinforce the 'one constant, not twenty' narrative.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a cross-reference to Part 4.6 code_analysis findings: 'This structural analysis is confirmed by per-WIN code review (Part 4.6): of 58 WINs reviewed, only 4 derive their predictions from dome geometry; the remaining 54 adopt values from standard physics or observations.' This creates a data-backed reinforcement of the section's central argument.

holes_found.description: THE '12 OF 14' COUNT IN THE KEY COLUMN PARAGRAPH IS POTENTIALLY CONFUSING. The paragraph says: 'In 12 of 14 cases, both models predict the same observable — making them non-discriminating. In #2, Tesla's own measurement assumes a spherical Earth. In #8, the dome's prediction contradicts the data.' So 12 + 1 + 1 = 14, which works. But the bottom line then says 'Zero of 20 domains produce a result where the globe disagrees and the dome uniquely explains the observation,' which applies to ALL 20 including Groups B and C. The transition from '14' to '20' is slightly abrupt — the key column paragraph covers Group A only, then the bottom line covers all groups without transition.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a bridging sentence before the bottom line: 'Combining all three groups...' or simply note which groups contribute to the final tally.

holes_found.description: DOMAIN #5 (EM-GRAVITY COUPLING) — 'Globe Predicts Same?' says 'YES (predicts 0.0)' but this is semantically ambiguous. The globe doesn't 'predict' zero EM-gravity coupling in the same sense as making a positive prediction — it simply has no mechanism for such coupling. Zero is the null expectation, not a derived prediction. A dome defender could say: 'The globe doesn't predict anything about EM-gravity coupling because it doesn't even have a framework for it. The dome makes a specific prediction (κ = 1.67 nT/μGal) that can be tested.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change to 'YES (null: 0.0)' or 'Globe: no mechanism → expect 0.0' to clarify this is a null expectation rather than a derived prediction. This is a small precision improvement.

stronger_arguments.description: CROSS-VALIDATION TEST. The section could propose a simple cross-validation: fit λ_g to domains 1-7, then test whether it correctly predicts domains 8-14. If the dome model's framework is genuinely unified, this out-of-sample test should succeed. If the section pointed out that no such cross-validation was performed, it would strengthen the 'tautology' argument significantly.
sources: Standard practice in model validation; see Hastie et al., Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 7

stronger_arguments.description: EFFECTIVE DEGREES OF FREEDOM CALCULATION. The section could estimate what the corrected significance would be if the 14 correlated domains were treated as one effective test. With 1 effective test from Group A + 3 from Group B (if generous) + 3 from Group C = ~7 at most. The 9.2σ assumed 20 independent domains; with ~7 effective degrees of freedom, the significance drops dramatically. A back-of-envelope calculation would make the 'meaningless' claim concrete rather than rhetorical.
sources: Chi-square degrees of freedom correction for correlated tests

stronger_arguments.description: THE DOME'S OWN OPEN PROBLEMS CONCEDE THE POINT. OPEN-001 states the dome needs 'dome-native coordinates without WGS84' — meaning the dome's coordinate system currently depends on the globe's. If the dome can't even locate cities without borrowing globe coordinates, the idea that its 20 domains are 'derived from first principles' with 'locked constants' is directly contradicted by the dome's own admission. This OPEN-001 argument should be cross-referenced in Section 3.1.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model HAS created a unified mathematical framework that applies a single set of equations across multiple physical domains. This is genuinely what good physics aspires to — derive many predictions from few parameters. The ambition of connecting geomagnetic, Schumann, gravity, and atmospheric phenomena through a single scale constant mirrors how fundamental constants (G, c, ℏ) unify disparate physics domains. The presentation as a multi-domain convergence dashboard reflects real scientific methodology in principle.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The difference is directionality. In standard physics, fundamental constants are measured in one domain and then make novel, testable predictions in unrelated domains (G from laboratory experiments → planetary orbits → gravitational waves). The dome's λ_g was fitted to geomagnetic/Schumann/gravity data and then 'tested' against the same kinds of data. There is no example of λ_g, derived from Domain X, making a surprising, confirmed prediction in unrelated Domain Y. The unification is retrospective, not predictive. A constant that explains everything it was fitted to explains nothing new.

deeper_analysis:
Section 3.1 is the structural backbone of Part 3 and arguably the single most important argument in the entire review after the kill-shots. If the 9.2σ claim stands, the dome model looks scientifically rigorous regardless of individual WIN failures. If it falls, the model's statistical credibility collapses.

The section's core logic is sound: shared parameters destroy the independence assumption required for aggregating significance across domains. The 14/20 classification is plausible based on the dome model's equations. The ruler analogy is effective for lay readers.

However, the section has a significant unforced error (Polaris inconsistency) and a structural vulnerability (no archived source for the Live Power page). The Polaris issue is the most urgent because it's in the most quotable paragraph — a dome defender could use a screenshot of our bottom line + our table entry to say 'they can't even keep their own analysis consistent.' The missing raw text is a process issue that should be fixed to make the section's domain classifications verifiable.

The deeper strategic issue is that the section's 'fitted constant' framing, while correct, is presented too simply. The strongest version of the argument combines: (1) λ_g was fitted to data from these domains, (2) no out-of-sample prediction exists, (3) the effective degrees of freedom are ~7 not 20, and (4) even among the 6 remaining domains, the globe predicts the same results. Currently, the section states (1) and (4) but doesn't develop (2) or (3). Adding the cross-validation and degrees-of-freedom arguments would make the section airtight.

On the other hand, I should note that a dome defender's strongest counter-argument would be: 'You've shown the domains share a parameter, but you haven't shown they were fitted to the SAME data. If λ_g was derived from geomagnetic pole data (Domain 3) and then correctly predicts Schumann resonance (Domain 1), that IS a cross-domain prediction even though both use λ_g.' This is a legitimate point that the section should address more carefully. The key rebuttal: the H(r) curve was fitted to THREE data points including Schumann-derived height (~9,500 km), so Schumann IS in the fitting set. The section should enumerate which specific data went into the λ_g fit.

citations_unchecked: The section references 'the Live Power page' but we have no archived copy to verify against
The 9.2σ and p = 1.2 × 10⁻²⁰ values are attributed to the dome's dashboard but cannot be verified without the archived page
The classification of all 20 domains and their λ_g dependencies relies on analysis of the dome's equations but the source equations are not directly cited
